name: Bulgogi Tacos (Korean-Mexican Fusion)
why: Korean-Mexican fusion is having a moment, and this recipe uses an Asian pear in the bulgogi marinade — a technique Martin will appreciate. Quick 10-minute cook time after marinating, perfect for a summer weeknight.
author: Henry
ingredients: 1 small Asian pear (for the marinade — it tenderizes and adds natural sweetness)
1½ lbs tender beef (top sirloin, thinly sliced against the grain)

steps: In a food processor, combine the Asian pear, onion, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, brown sugar, black pepper, and sesame oil. Blend until smooth — this is your bulgogi marinade.
Add the thinly sliced beef to the marinade, tossing to coat every piece evenly.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight for maximum flavor. The longer it sits, the more tender and flavorful the beef will be.
Heat a cast iron skillet over high heat until searing hot.
Add 2 tablespoons of vegetable oil to the skillet.
Lay the marinated beef in a single layer — do not overcrowd the pan. Cook in about 3 batches.
Fry until the beef is crisp and caramelized on one side, about 1-2 minutes.
Flip and fry the other side for another 1-2 minutes until done.
Transfer cooked beef to a plate and repeat with remaining batches.
Toast the corn tortillas directly over a gas flame or in a dry skillet until warm and slightly charred — about 30 seconds per side.
Layer shredded cabbage on each warm tortilla.
Top with the cooked bulgogi beef.
Add chopped kimchi, fresh cilantro, a dollop of sour cream, and crumbled queso fresco.
Squeeze fresh lime juice over the top and serve immediately.

pro_tips: The Asian pear in the marinade is non-negotiable — it contains natural enzymes (bromelain) that tenderize the meat while adding subtle sweetness. If unavailable, pineapple works as a substitute.
For extra crispy beef, pat the marinated beef dry with paper towels before searing. Moisture is the enemy of the Maillard reaction.
